elements.sent-2411-001: Utilizing temporal information to improve the performance of 3D detection has made great progress recently in the field of autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-002: Traditional transformer-based temporal fusion methods suffer from quadratic computational cost and information decay as the length of the frame sequence increases.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we propose a novel method called MambaDETR, whose main idea is to implement temporal fusion in the efficient state space.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, we design a Motion Elimination module to remove the relatively static objects for temporal fusion.
elements.sent-2411-005: On the standard nuScenes benchmark, our proposed MambaDETR achieves remarkable result in the 3D object detection task, exhibiting state-of-the-art performance among existing temporal fusion methods.
elements.sent-2411-006: Multi-view 3D object detection is a fundamental task in autonomous driving, enabling vehicles to perceive their surrounding environment using sensor data.
elements.sent-2411-007: Recent studies have leveraged temporal information from image frame sequences to improve detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-008: Transformers \cite{vaswani2017attention with attention mechanisms have proven effective in modeling dependencies for sequential inputs, leading many approaches to adopt transformer-based temporal fusion methods to explore temporal information for 3D detection.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Existing transformer-based temporal fusion methods, such as \cite{liu2023petrv2, luo2022detr4d , use one adjacent historical frame to interact with the current frame in the transformer decoder, which can efficiently improve 3D detection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, these methods suffer from a quadratic increase in computational cost as the sequence length grows, limiting their ability to adopt more frames for temporal interaction.
elements.sent-2411-011: To solve this problem, subsequent methods \cite{lin2022sparse4d, wang2023exploring, li2022bevformer, cai2022memot, he2022queryprop, zeng2022motr, meinhardt2022trackformer, jiang2019video incorporate multiple frames into the temporal fusion module by fusing frames in a recurrent (See Figure \ref{cover a) rather than a sequential manner.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The long-term historical information is propagated frame by frame, allowing each frame’s features to integrate information from preceding frames.
elements.sent-2411-013: However, this recurrent fusion process can cause information decay over time, leading the model to focus more on current information rather than long-term frame \cite{lin2022sparse4d, li2022bevformer .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=.98\linewidth]{images/Cover.png} \caption{ Different temporal fusion methods in recurrent manner and sequential manner. } \label{cover}
elements.sent-2411-014: To address these challenges, we propose a novel method called MambaDETR, which represents temporal fusion candidates as 3D queries initialized from 2D proposals and performs temporal fusion in a hidden space using an SSM-based (Structured State Space Model) module, as shown in Figure \ref{cover (b).}
elements.sent-2411-015: This approach replaces the traditional transformer-based module with an SSM-based module, enabling temporal fusion in a sequential manner that effectively models long-range information while maintaining only linear memory and computation costs.
elements.sent-2411-016: Specifically, given a sequence of frames, we first employ a 2D detector to independently generate high-quality 2D proposals for each frame.
elements.sent-2411-017: These proposals are then used to produce 3D object queries through 3D projection, as in previous methods \cite{jiang2024far3d , resulting in a sequence of 3D object queries at each time step.}
elements.sent-2411-018: Furthermore, according to the physical laws of motion, the same 3D object does not significantly shift between adjacent frames.
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, fusing all the queries from the adjacent frames can lead to unnecessary computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-020: Based on this insight, we introduce a Motion Elimination Module, which aligns the objects from the previous frame to the current frame through ego transformation and generates a motion mask based on the relative movements of objects.
elements.sent-2411-021: Consequently, trivial and relative static objects in the ego vehicle coordinate are removed from the previous frames, retaining only the moving objects and thereby enabling more efficient temporal fusion.
elements.sent-2411-022: The refined sequence of 3D object queries is then input into the Query Mamba Module, which performs query-based temporal fusion in the state space.
elements.sent-2411-023: By leveraging a structured state space layer, the Query Mamba Module achieves long-range modeling without pairwise comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-024: Consequently, MambaDETR can be efficiently applied to long-range sequences of image frames.
elements.sent-2411-026: A novel temporal fusion method called MambaDETR is proposed for 3D object detection, which achieves efficient temporal fusion in the state space.
elements.sent-2411-027: This approach sequentially fuses frame sequences, thereby fully exploiting long-range information while avoid quadratic complexity.
elements.sent-2411-028: The Motion Elimination module is designed, which removes relatively static objects while retaining moving ones in the ego vehicle coordinates, thereby improving fusion efficiency and reducing computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-029: Comprehensive experiments have been conducted on the standard nuScenes dataset, and the evaluation results demonstrate the superior performance of MambaDETR in 3D object detection, with linear computation cost compared to transformer-based methods, e.g. StreamPETR.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Temporal Modeling in Multi-view 3D Object Detection
elements.sent-2411-030: Multi-view 3D detection is a crucial task in autonomous driving, requiring the processing of multiple camera images and the prediction of 3D bounding boxes.
elements.sent-2411-031: Pioneer's research~\cite{wang2022detr3d,petr_liu_2022,huang2021bevdet,li2022bevformer,jiang2023polarformer,wang2023object centers on the effective conversion of various perspective views into a cohesive 3D space within a single frame.}
elements.sent-2411-032: The transformation process can be categorized into two groups: methods based on dense BEV (Bird's Eye View) representation ~\cite{huang2021bevdet,li2022bevformer,huang2023fast,li2023bevdepth,jiang2023polarformer, liu2023bevfusion, liang2022bevfusion and methods based on sparse queries~\cite{wang2022detr3d,petr_liu_2022,lin2022sparse4d,wang2023object .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Recent studies~\cite{li2022bevformer,sts_wang_2022,huang2022bevdet4d,park2022time,lin2022sparse4d,luo2022detr4d,cape_xiong_2023,liBevstereoEnhancingDepth2023,wang2023exploring have incorporated temporal information to address the occlusion issue and enhance speed prediction accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-034: BEVFormer \cite{li2022bevformer first introduces sequential temporal modeling into multi-view 3D object detection and applies temporal self-attention.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The BEVDet4D \cite{huang2022bevdet4d paradigm is proposed to be lifted from the spatial-only 3D space to the temporal 4D space.}
elements.sent-2411-036: For the first time, vision-based methods become comparable with those using radar or LiDAR.
elements.sent-2411-037: Sparse4D \cite{lin2022sparse4d refines anchor boxes iteratively by incorporating sparse spatial-temporal fusion to improve sparse 3D detection.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Additionally, DETR4D \cite{luo2022detr4d introduces a novel hybrid approach that performs cross-frame fusion over past object queries and image features, enabling efficient and robust modeling of temporal information.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Recently, StreamPETR \cite{wang2023exploring efficiently models temporal data and object tracking through frame-by-frame query propagation and motion-aware layer normalization.}
elements.sent-2411-040: State space models have emerged as a promising alternative to traditional sequence modeling approaches.
elements.sent-2411-041: One key issue with traditional attention mechanisms~\cite{vaswani2017attention,dao2022flashattention,dao2023flashattention,peng2023rwkv , is their quadratic time and space complexity in relation to sequence length.}
elements.sent-2411-042: To overcome this limitation, \cite{combining_gu_2021 proposed the Linear State-Space Layer (LSSL), a model inspired by control systems that combines recurrent neural networks, temporal convolutions, and neural differential equations.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Further advancements in state space models include the introduction of the S5 layer \cite{simplified_smith_2022 , exploring the use of Gated State Spaces for long-range language modeling \cite{long_mehta_2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Recently, the generic language model backbone, Mamba \cite{gu2023mamba , outperforms Transformers at various sizes on large-scale real data and enjoys linear scaling in sequence length.}
elements.sent-2411-045: These advancements have been integrated into larger representation models \cite{ma2022mega,fu2022hungry,raw_goel_2022,selective_wang_2023,localmamba_huang_2024 , further demonstrating the versatility and scalability of structured state space models in various applications.}
elements.sent-2411-046: State space models have also been extended in visual tasks.
elements.sent-2411-047: TranS4mer \cite{islam2023efficient combines the strengths of S4 and self-attention, achieving state-of-the-art performance for movie scene detection.}
elements.sent-2411-048: And VMamba \cite{liu2024vmamba introduces a vision backbone with linear time complexity that integrates Visual State-Space (VSS) blocks with the 2D Selective Scan (SS2D) module.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/pipeline.png} \caption{ Overall architecture of the proposed MambaDETR. Key enhancements include 2D-priors-based query initialization, Motion Elimination to retain only moving 3D queries across frames, and Query Mamba for state-space temporal fusion. The 3D queries interact with the current image frame in a transformer decoder, producing the final 3D object detections. } \label{pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-049: Figure \ref{pipeline illustrates the overall structure of the proposed MambaDETR, which follows the architecture of DETR3D \cite{wang2022detr3d , where objects are represented as queries extracted from multi-view image features.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: MambaDETR develop DETR3D with the following designs: 2D-priors-based query initialization (Section \ref{Query Generator ), Motion Elimination (Section \ref{Motion Elimination ), and Query Mamba (Section \ref{Query Mamba ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: For the 2D-priors-based query initialization, we input image features and leverage a 2D detector to obtain 2D proposals.
elements.sent-2411-052: The 3D queries are then initialized from these 2D proposals through 3D projection.
elements.sent-2411-053: The Motion Elimination Module removes redundant, motionless 3D queries from previous frames, retaining only moving 3D queries for temporal fusion.
elements.sent-2411-054: For the Query Mamba, we leverage the 3D query sequence from multiple time steps as input and realize temporal fusion in the state-space.
elements.sent-2411-055: After that, the output queries are interact with current image frame in the transformer decoder and generate final 3D prediction.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/Motion_Elimination.png} \caption{ Detail structure of Query Generator and Motion Elimination modules in the MambaDETR architecture. (a) The Query Generator utilizes a 2D detector and DepthNet to create 2D proposals, which are transformed into 3D queries by integrating position and semantic embeddings. (b) The Motion Elimination module then filters out static 3D queries across frames by measuring the distance between the center points. } \label{Motion+query}
elements.sent-2411-056: Previous method \cite{xie2023sparsefusion generate numerous queries by applying maximum pooling operations on image heatmaps, which is inefficient and computationally expensive.}
elements.sent-2411-057: To address this issue, existing query-based 3D object detection methods \cite{jiang2024far3d, wang2023object, ji2024enhancing have introduced 2D proposals to enhance 3D detection performance.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Inspired by these methods, the proposed 2D Priors-based Query Generator uses a 2D object detector to localize objects within specific regions.
elements.sent-2411-059: As a result, the 2D detector not only improves computational efficiency by reducing the number of queries but also provides valuable 2D priors for localizing objects in 3D space.
elements.sent-2411-060: To the best of our knowledge, MambaDETR is the first 3D object detection method that leverages a 2D detector to enhance temporal fusion performance.
elements.sent-2411-061: Specifically, given the image features after the image backbone, we feed them into the Faster-RCNN detector \cite{wang2023object and a lightweight depth estimation network \cite{li2023bevdepth , resulting in a set of 2D bounding boxes and depth distributions.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The 2D detector head follows the original design, while the depth distribution is represented in discretized bins \cite{jiang2024far3d .}
elements.sent-2411-063: Given each 2D bounding box in view $i$, we initialize the 2D query $\mathbf{Q _\text{2D}$ from the center point $\mathbf{c _{\text{2D =(c_\text{w , c_\text{h )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: To aggregate semantic information of the 2D bounding box, we interact the $\mathbf{Q _\text{2D}$ with the surrounding pixels through deformable attention, following the similar process in \cite{zhu2020deformable, xia2022vision, li2023dfa3d, liang2022recurrent , to obtain the semantic embedding $\mathbf{Q _\text{sem}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The pixel candidates are chosen based on the location of the query and the sampling offsets from the image feature $\mathbf{F}$ in view $i$.
elements.sent-2411-066: The overall process is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\operatorname{DeformAttn}(Q_2D, c_{2D}, F) = \sum_{m = 1}^{N_{head}} W_m\sum_{n = 1}^{N_{key}} A_{mn} W'_{m} F (c_{2D} + \Delta c_{mn}),\end{equation} where $\mathbf{Q}$, $\mathbf{c _{\text{2D }$, and $\mathbf{F}$ represent the query, reference point, and image features, respectively.}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\operatorname{DeformAttn}(Q_2D, c_{2D}, F) = \sum_{m = 1}^{N_{head}} W_m\sum_{n = 1}^{N_{key}} A_{mn} W'_{m} F (c_{2D} + \Delta c_{mn}),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-067: The index $m$ denotes the attention head, and $N_{\text{head }$ is the total number of attention heads.}
elements.sent-2411-068: The index $n$ corresponds to the sampled key, and $N_{\text{key }$ is the total number of sampled keys for each head.}
elements.sent-2411-069: The vector $\Delta \mathbf{c _{mn \in \mathbb{R ^2$ represents the predicted offsets to the reference point $\mathbf{c _{\text{2D }$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: The term $\mathbf{F (\mathbf{c _{\text{2D + \Delta \mathbf{c _{mn )$ represents the feature at location $\mathbf{c _{\text{2D + \Delta \mathbf{c _{mn}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: To further lift the 2D query into the 3D space, the center point $\mathbf{c _{\text{2D =(c_\text{w , c_\text{h )$ of each 2d bounding box is combined with the corresponding predicted depth distribution $\mathbf{d _{\text{c }$ and project to the 3D center points $\mathbf{c _{\text{3D }$ of 3D proposals: \begin{equation}\mathbf{c}_{\text{3D}} = \mathbf{K}^{-1}_j \mathbf{I}^{-1}_j [\mathbf{c}_\text{w} \ast \mathbf{d}_{\text{c}}, \mathbf{c}_\text{h} \ast \mathbf{d}_{\text{c}}, \mathbf{d}_{\text{c}}, \mathbf{1}]^T,\end{equation} where $\mathbf{K _j$, $\mathbf{I _j$ denote extrinsic and intrinsic matrices of the $j$-th camera.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}c_{3D} = K^{-1}_j I^{-1}_j [c_w \ast d_{c}, c_h \ast d_{c}, d_{c}, 1]^T,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-072: After generating center points $\mathbf{c _{\text{3D }$ of the 3D proposals, we use sinusoidal transformation and MLP to obtain the 3D position embedding $\mathbf{Q _\text{pos}$ following the process and combine with the aforementioned $\mathbf{Q _\text{sem}$ together and result in 3D query $\mathbf{Q _{\text{3D }$: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{Q}_{\text{pos}} = \operatorname{PosEmbed}(\mathbf{c}_{\text{3D}}), \mathbf{Q}_{\text{3D}} = \mathbf{Q}_{\text{pos}} + \mathbf{Q}_{\text{sem}}. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The Motion Elimination (ME) module is applied optimize the computation cost by discarding the relative static object queries for the subsequent temporal fusion.
elements.sent-2411-074: In more detail, we present the structure of ME module in Figure \ref{Motion+query b, which consists of three parts: zero padding, frame alignment.}
elements.sent-2411-075: For the zero padding part, given the 3D queries sequence $\mathbf{Q ^\text{seq _{\text{3D = [\mathbf{Q ^{t _{\text{3D , \mathbf{Q ^{t-1 _{\text{3D , \cdots ,\mathbf{Q ^{t-N _{\text{3D ]$, we select the 3D queries with the in the $i-th$ frame $\mathbf{Q ^i_{\text{3D }$ that have the largest number of 3D proposal and represented as $K$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Then we add zero padding to all the 3D queries in the remaining frames and get the updated 3D queries sequence $\mathbf{\hat{Q ^\text{seq _{\text{3D = [\mathbf{\hat{Q ^{t _{\text{3D , \mathbf{\hat{Q ^{t-1 _{\text{3D , \cdots ,\mathbf{\hat{Q ^{t-N _{\text{3D ]$ with size of $K \times N$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: For the frame alignment, we select the current queries $\mathbf{\hat{Q ^{t _{\text{3D }$, previous queries in the $t$-$j$ frame $\mathbf{\hat{Q ^{t-j _{\text{3D }$ and their corresponding 3D center points $\mathbf{C ^{t _{\text{3D }$, $\mathbf{C ^{t-j _{\text{3D }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Then the previous query $\mathbf{\hat{Q ^{t-j _{\text{3D }$ is aligned to the current frame through ego transformation, which following the process in \cite{hou2024query .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Given the ego pose matrix of $\mathbf{R ^t_{w}$ and $\mathbf{R ^{t-j _{w}$ in the current and $t$-$j$ frame, we first compute the ego transformation matrix and align the center of the objects in the $t$-$j$ frame to the current frame.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: The overall process is formulated as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{R}^t_{t-j} = \mathbf{R}^t_{\text{w}} \cdot \operatorname{inv}(\mathbf{R}^{t-j}_{w}), \mathbf{C}'_{t-1} = (\mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{V}_{t-j} \cdot \Delta t) \cdot (\mathbf{R}^t_{t-j}) ^T. \end{aligned}\end{equation} After getting the aligned center points, we utilize the L2 center distance of object and create cost matrix $\mathbf{D ^t_{t-j \in \mathbb{R ^{K \times K}$ to measure the moving distance of objects between the chosen frames. \begin{equation}\mathbf{D}^t_{t-j} = \mathbf{L}_2 (\mathbf{C}_{t} - \mathbf{C}'_{t-1}) \\\end{equation} Then we distinguish the relative static position of the objects with two standards: The L2 distance of object is below the threshold $\alpha$ and the category of the objects in frame $t$ and $t$-$j$ are the same. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{M}_{t-j} = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll} 0, D^t_{t-j} \leq \alpha \text{ and } \text{CAT}_t = \text{CAT}_{t-j} 1e^6, D^t_{t-j} > \alpha \text{ or } \text{CAT}_t \neq \text{CAT}_{t-j} \end{array} \right., \mathbf{A}_{t-j} = \operatorname{softmax}(\mathbf{M}_{t-j}), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{A _{t-j \in \mathbb{R ^{K \times K}$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation} M_{t-j} = \left\{ {ll} 0, D^t_{t-j} \leq \alpha  and  CAT_t = CAT_{t-j} 1e^6, D^t_{t-j} > \alpha  or  CAT_t \neq CAT_{t-j}  \right., A_{t-j} = \operatorname{softmax}(M_{t-j}), }\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: We identify the object of $t$-$j$ frame is relative static if it has one relative static relationship in the $t$ frame.
elements.sent-2411-082: The process is formulated as: \begin{equation}B^{n}_j = \begin{cases} 1 \text{if } \sum_{m=1}^{K} \mathbf{A}^{mn}_{t-j} = K 0 \text{otherwise} \end{cases}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-083: After that, we can obtain the motion mask of the $t$-$j$ frame $\mathbf{B _{t-j = \{ B^{n _{t-j \}^{K _{n=1 \in \mathbb{R ^{1 \times K}$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: We extend the above process to all the previous frames and gather the motion mask $\mathbf{B = [\mathbf{B ^T_t, \mathbf{B ^T_{t-1 , \cdots , \mathbf{B ^T_{t-N ]$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: To be notice that we set all the elements of $\mathbf{B _t$ to be 1, which means all the queries of the current frame will be remained.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Finally, we multiply the 3D queries sequence with the motion mask and get the relative moving query sequence: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{Q}_{\text{moving}} = \mathbf{\hat{Q}}^\text{seq}_{\text{3D}} \ast \mathbf{B} = [\mathbf{\hat{Q}}^{t}_{\text{3D}}, \mathbf{\hat{Q}}^{t-1}_{\text{3D}}, \cdots ,\mathbf{\hat{Q}}^{t-N}_{\text{3D}}] \ast [\mathbf{B}^T_t, \mathbf{B}^T_{t-1}, \cdots , \mathbf{B}^T_{t-N}]. \end{aligned}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-087: The Structured State-Space Model (SSM) can be regarded as linear-time-invariant system that map the input stimulation $x(t)$ to the response $y(t)$ through the hidden state $h(t) \in \mathbb{R ^{N}$.}
elements.sent-2411-088: The SSM can be defined in the continuous time using the following equations: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{h}'(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{h}(t) + \mathbf{B}x(t), y(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{h}(t) + Dx(t), \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\mathbf{A \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times N , \mathbf{B \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times 1 , \mathbf{C \in \mathbb{R ^{1 \times N}$ and $D \in \mathbb{R ^{1}$ are the weighting parameters.}}}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation} h'(t) = Ah(t) + Bx(t), y(t) = Ch(t) + Dx(t), \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-089: To apply the continuous SSM into the deep models, we introduce a timescale parameter $\Delta$ and discretize the SSM as follow: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{h}_k = \bar{\mathbf{A}} \mathbf{h}_{k-1} + \bar{\mathbf{B}} \mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{y}_k = \bar{\mathbf{C}} \mathbf{h}_k + \bar{D} \mathbf{x}_k, \label{eq:DSSM} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\bar{\mathbf{A , \bar{\mathbf{B , \bar{\mathbf{C }$ and $\bar{D}$ are the discrete version of $\mathbf{A , \mathbf{B , \mathbf{C}$ and $D$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation} h_k = \bar{A} h_{k-1} + \bar{B} x_k, y_k = \bar{C} h_k + \bar{D} x_k, \label{eq:DSSM} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-090: The equation \eqref{eq:DSSM can also be expressed as a convolution operation: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bar{\mathbf{K}} = \left( \bar{\mathbf{C}}\bar{\mathbf{B}}, \bar{\mathbf{C}}\bar{\mathbf{A}}\bar{\mathbf{B}}, \ldots, \bar{\mathbf{C}}\bar{\mathbf{A}}^{L-1}\bar{\mathbf{B}} \right), \mathbf{y} = \bar{\mathbf{K}} * \mathbf{x}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $\bar{\mathbf{K }$ is convolution kernel and $L$ is the length of the input sequence.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The overview of the proposed Query Mamba is shown in Figure \ref{pipeline .}
elements.sent-2411-092: For the queries $\mathbf{\hat{Q ^{t-i _{\text{3D }$ in the $t$-$i$ frame, we concatenate all the queries along the channel dimension $D$ and get the fusion candidate $\mathbf{Q ^{t-i _{\text{moving \in \mathbb{R ^{1 \times KD}$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: After that, following the standard 1-D input of Mamba, we send the fusion candidate sequence $\mathbf{Q _{\text{moving = \{ \mathbf{Q ^{t-i _{\text{moving \}^{N _{i=1 \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times KD}$ into the Query Mamba module.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: First, we pass the sequence through the Layer Normalization layer.
elements.sent-2411-095: Subsequently, we apply a depth-wise convolution (DW Conv) along with a residual connection, which improves the efficiency of the CNN and has been used in VMamba \cite{liu2024vmamba .}
elements.sent-2411-096: Afterward, we apply another Layer Normalization and a Gated S4 (GS4) layer, also with a residual connection.
elements.sent-2411-097: Finally, the sequence is passed through a Linear layer with a residual connection that connects from the initial input of Query Mamba: \begin{equation}z = \operatorname{DWConv}(\operatorname{LN}(Q_{moving}))+\operatorname{LN}(Q_{moving}), z' = \operatorname{GS4}(\operatorname{LN}(z))+\operatorname{LN}(z), Q_{out} = \operatorname{Linear}(z') + Q_{moving}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-098: The SSM-based model plays a vital role in our Mamba DETR method, so as the cross-attention in the transformer-based temporal fusion methods \cite{wang2023exploring, li2022bevformer .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Given the input sequence $\mathbf{Q _{\text{moving = \{ \mathbf{Q ^{t-i _{\text{moving \}^{N _{i=1 \in \mathbb{R ^{N \times KD}$, the computational complexity of cross-attention and SSM is formulated as: \begin{equation}\Omega(cross-attention) = 4N(KD)^2 + 2N^2KD, \Omega(SSM) = 3N(2D)M + N(2KD)M,\end{equation} where $M$ is a fixed parameter set to 16.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: As we can see, the computational complexity of cross-attention is quadratic in the sequence length $N$, while the SSM is linear in $N$.
elements.sent-2411-101: This computational advantage makes the SSM-based method scalable for long sequence modeling and thus beneficial for long-range exploration.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Ablation study on different components of MambaDETR}\label{ablation} 1{ \begin{tabular}{ c c c c| c c } 2D detector SE ME QMamba NDS mAP \tikzxmark \tikzxmark \tikzxmark \tikzxmark 57.5 49.4 \tikzcmark \tikzxmark \tikzxmark \tikzxmark 58.1 49.8 \tikzcmark \tikzcmark \tikzxmark \tikzxmark 58.3 50.4 \tikzcmark \tikzcmark \tikzcmark \tikzxmark 58.6 50.5 \tikzcmark \tikzcmark \tikzcmark \tikzcmark 60.5 50.8 \end{tabular}}

elements.sent-2411-102: The nuScenes dataset is a comprehensive resource for 3D object detection, encompassing 1,000 scenes, each lasting about 20 seconds and annotated at a frequency of 2 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-103: This dataset features images captured from six cameras, alongside data from five radars and one LiDAR system, providing a complete 360° field of view.
elements.sent-2411-104: The annotations include up to 1.4 million 3D bounding boxes across ten categories: car, truck, bus, trailer, construction vehicle, pedestrian, motorcycle, bicycle, barrier, and traffic cone.
elements.sent-2411-105: The scenes are organized into training (700), validation (150), and testing (150) sets.
elements.sent-2411-106: For evaluation, we utilize several metrics, including the mean Average Precision (mAP), nuScenes Detection Score (NDS), and various True Positive metrics such as Average Translation Error (ATE) and Average Velocity Error (AVE).
elements.sent-2411-107: The mAP is determined based on the distance between 2D centers on the ground plane, and NDS provides a holistic measure of detection performance by aggregating other relevant indicators.
elements.sent-2411-108: We analyze the impact of the 2D detector and the Semantic Aggregation (SE) module on generating object queries, as shown in Table \ref{ablation and Table \ref{ablation_query .}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Specifically, adding the 2D detector for query generation achieves significant improvements, with mAP increasing by 0.6\% and NDS increasing by 0.4\%.
elements.sent-2411-110: This result indicates that the 2D detector can provide reliable 2D priors for the query generator and thus benefit 3D object detection.
elements.sent-2411-111: Besides, it can also be seen that the 2D detector negatively impacts inference speed and GPU memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-112: We argue that the additional computational cost introduced by the 2D detector outweighs the savings from the reduced number of queries.
elements.sent-2411-113: Semantic aggregation is also proven to be effective in improving the quality of queries, indicating the importance of semantic information in query generation.
elements.sent-2411-114: MambaDETR reduces the non-trivial queries through the Motion Elimination (ME) module.
elements.sent-2411-115: Table \ref{ablation_query shows that the ME module can significantly improve inference speed by 1.2 FPS and reduce GPU memory usage by 0.6 GB, which indicates that the ME module successfully removes the non-vital queries.}
elements.sent-2411-116: Furthermore, the proposed ME module also achieves a limited improvement of 0.1\% in mAP and 0.3\% in NDS.
elements.sent-2411-117: This indicates that reducing redundant queries can not only improve computational efficiency but also benefit detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-118: We experiment with different S4 variants (Table \ref{S4_type ) and the impact of varying the number of layers (Table \ref{S4_number ).}}
elements.sent-2411-119: In Table \ref{S4_type , we evaluate several S4 variants, including the standard S4, S6, Diagonal S4 (DS4), and Gated S4 (GS4).}
elements.sent-2411-120: Our findings indicate that GS6 achieves the highest mAP of 50.8, outperforming the other variants.
elements.sent-2411-121: In Table \ref{S4_number , we assess the effect of increasing the number of S4 layers on mAP.}
elements.sent-2411-122: The results demonstrate that performance improves steadily with additional layers, stabilizing at an mAP of 50.8 with 6 layers.
elements.sent-2411-123: The result indicates that GS4 model with 6 layer can achieves the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-124: We compare the proposed Mamba DETR with previous state-of-the-art vision-based 3D detectors on the nuScenes validation and test sets.
elements.sent-2411-125: As shown in Table \ref{benchmark_val , Mamba DETR demonstrates superior performance on the validation set, achieving significant improvements in NDS, mAP, and localization metrics compared to other methods.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Specifically, with a ResNet101 backbone and 8-frame input, Mamba DETR outperforms StreamPETR by 0.7\% in NDS and 0.4\% in mAP.
elements.sent-2411-127: The mATE value of Mamba DETR also shows notable improvements, reducing the localization error compared to most competitors.
elements.sent-2411-128: This indicates the efficacy of Mamba DETR's object detection and tracking capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-129: When comparing the performance on the test set in Table \ref{benchmark_test , Mamba DETR again achieves impressive results.}
elements.sent-2411-130: With a ViT-Large backbone, Mamba DETR reaches an mAP of 68.2\% and an NDS of 60.7\%, surpassing StreamPETR by 0.6\% in mAP and demonstrating comparable performance in mASE and mAOE metrics.
elements.sent-2411-131: Notably, Mamba DETR also achieves lower mAVE compared to many competing methods, suggesting improved velocity estimation and better temporal consistency for tracking moving objects.
elements.sent-2411-132: Besides, we show the visualization of the detection results In Figure \ref{visualization , which also effectively demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed MambaDETR.}
elements.sent-2411-133: To validate long-term exploration of input image sequence, we evaluate both StreamPETR and MambaDETR with different numbers of training frames (i.e., 1, 2, 4, 8, and 12).
elements.sent-2411-134: The results are presented in Figure \ref{line-chart (a).}
elements.sent-2411-135: First, we notice that a short temporal window of 2 training frames produces a suboptimal performance of 40.7\% mAP.
elements.sent-2411-136: Expanding the temporal range to 8 frames boosts the performance to 50.8\% mAP, reflecting a 10.1\% improvement.
elements.sent-2411-137: Extending further to 12 frames continues to enhance the mAP, reaching 52.2\%, which indicates that adding more frames does not obtain promising improvements.
elements.sent-2411-138: Compared to our baseline, StreamPETR, MambaDETR consistently outperforms in terms of mAP across all training frames, particularly excelling with an extended temporal window.
elements.sent-2411-139: This demonstrates the superior capability of our proposed method to leverage long-range temporal information, resulting in significantly better detection performance.
elements.sent-2411-140: In Figure \ref{line-chart b and c, we analyze the computational efficiency of the MambaDETR in two aspect: inference speed and memory cost.}
elements.sent-2411-141: In Figure \ref{line-chart b, we observe that the inference speed decreases as the sequence length increase from 35.9\% to 26.0\% in FPS.}
elements.sent-2411-142: Meanwhile, the inference speed of StreamPETR remains stable around 27\% due to the recurrent temporal fusion mechanism that keep the fusion candidates invariant to the sequence length.
elements.sent-2411-143: However, our MambaDETR still infer faster than StreamPETR except for the frame numbers equal to 12.
elements.sent-2411-144: Moreover, Figure \ref{line-chart c demonstrates that MambaDETR has significantly lower memory consumption compared to StreamPETR, with only linear memory increase as the sequence length grow.}
elements.sent-2411-145: With 12 frames, MambaDETR requires around 15 GB of memory, whereas StreamPETR’s memory requirement is considerably higher, peaking at nearly 40 GB.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/line-chart.png} \caption{ Performance(mAP), Inference Speed(samples/Second) and Memory Requirements(GB) of MambaDETR as the function of the sequence length. } \label{line-chart}
elements.sent-2411-146: In this paper, we propose MambaDETR, an effective 3D object detection framework for long-sequence temporal fusion.
elements.sent-2411-147: Unlike previous approaches, our method uses a state-space model (SSM) to perform efficient, sequential temporal fusion with linear memory and computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-148: Additionally, we introduce the Motion Elimination Module, which selectively retains only moving objects, enhancing fusion efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-149: MambaDETR achieves state-of-the-art performance on the nuScenes dataset, demonstrating significant improvements in computational efficiency over transformer-based methods while maintaining accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-150: We hope MambaDETR offers valuable insights for advancing long-sequence temporal modeling in autonomous driving applications.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-153: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-154: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-155: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-156: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-157: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-158: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-159: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-160: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-161: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-162: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-163: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-164: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-165: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-166: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-167: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-168: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-169: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-172: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-173: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-174: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-175: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-176: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-177: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-178: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-011: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-180: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-183: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-184: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-185: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-186: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-187: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
